Definition: This subdomain encompasses human conceptualization of the material composition of reality, including the fundamental building blocks of the physical world and their perceived properties. It captures how humans categorize and understand different types of matter based on observable characteristics, composition, and behavioral properties.
Exemplary Concepts: Metals (iron, gold, copper); Liquids (water, oil, blood); Gases (air, steam, smoke); Organic materials (wood, leather, bone); Minerals and stones (granite, quartz, clay); Composites (concrete, plastic, alloys); Powders and granules (sand, flour, salt); Biological substances (flesh, plant matter, bodily fluids)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of movement, positioning, and spatial relationships in the physical world. It encompasses both the static concept of where things are located and the dynamic concept of how things move through space, as perceived and conceptualized by human cognition.
Exemplary Concepts: Types of motion (walking, running, falling, rotating, vibrating); Directional movement (ascending, descending, approaching, retreating); Positional relationships (above, below, inside, adjacent, behind); Trajectories (straight line, curved path, circular motion, zigzag); Speed variations (accelerating, decelerating, constant velocity); Relative motion (following, chasing, orbiting, parallel movement); Positional states (stationary, balanced, tilted, suspended); Spatial boundaries (edges, surfaces, perimeters, centers)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses processes of physical change that alter the form, state, or properties of matter without necessarily changing its fundamental identity. It captures how humans understand and categorize the various ways physical entities can be modified, combined, or altered through natural or intentional processes.
Exemplary Concepts: State changes (melting, freezing, evaporation, condensation, sublimation); Mechanical transformations (breaking, cutting, grinding, shaping, molding); Thermal processes (heating, cooling, burning, charring); Chemical-physical changes (rusting, fermentation, decay, crystallization); Mixing and separation (dissolving, filtering, distillation, precipitation); Deformation (stretching, compressing, bending, twisting); Assembly and disassembly (construction, demolition, joining, splitting); Weathering processes (erosion, oxidation, wear, aging)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses large-scale physical events and processes that occur in the natural environment, typically beyond direct human control. It captures how humans perceive, categorize, and understand weather patterns, geological events, astronomical phenomena, and other manifestations of natural forces.
Exemplary Concepts: Weather phenomena (rain, snow, wind, storms, lightning, rainbows); Geological events (earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, landslides, tsunamis); Astronomical phenomena (sunrise, sunset, eclipses, meteor showers, tides); Seasonal patterns (spring blooming, autumn leaf change, winter dormancy); Atmospheric effects (fog, mist, aurora, mirages, optical illusions); Water phenomena (waterfalls, geysers, hot springs, floods, droughts); Fire phenomena (wildfires, spontaneous combustion, electrical discharges); Magnetic and electrical (magnetic fields, static electricity, natural radiation)

Definition: This subdomain captures the various ways humans physically engage with, modify, and control objects in their environment. It encompasses both the actions humans perform on objects and the cognitive understanding of how objects can be manipulated, used as tools, or arranged to achieve specific goals.
Exemplary Concepts: Basic manipulations (grasping, lifting, pushing, pulling, carrying, throwing); Tool use (cutting with knives, hammering, digging, measuring, writing); Fine motor control (threading, drawing, carving, assembling small parts); Object arrangement (stacking, organizing, aligning, sorting, grouping); Force application (pressing, squeezing, twisting, leverage, mechanical advantage); Construction activities (building, joining, fastening, weaving, molding); Destructive actions (breaking, tearing, crushing, dismantling); Precision operations (aiming, balancing, calibrating, adjusting)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human conceptualization of living beings and the fundamental processes that characterize life. It captures how humans categorize and understand organisms, their life cycles, and the essential activities that distinguish living from non-living entities, based on observable behaviors and intuitive biological knowledge.
Exemplary Concepts: Organisms by type (animals, plants, fungi, microorganisms, parasites); Life cycle stages (birth, growth, reproduction, aging, death); Vital processes (breathing, feeding, metabolism, circulation, excretion); Reproductive processes (mating, pregnancy, laying eggs, pollination, spawning); Growth and development (germination, maturation, molting, flowering); Species interactions (predation, symbiosis, competition, cooperation); Biological inheritance (genetics, heredity, family resemblance, breeding); Adaptation mechanisms (evolution, natural selection, survival strategies)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of the spectrum from wellness to disease, including the recognition, categorization, and management of health states. It encompasses both physical and mental health conditions as they are experienced, perceived, and culturally understood by humans, rather than purely medical definitions.
Exemplary Concepts: Health states (wellness, vitality, fitness, robustness, energy levels); Illness types (infections, injuries, chronic conditions, genetic disorders); Symptoms and signs (pain, fever, fatigue, inflammation, weakness); Mental health conditions (depression, anxiety, stress, cognitive decline); Recovery processes (healing, convalescence, rehabilitation, remission); Preventive measures (hygiene, exercise, nutrition, immunization, rest); Health threats (contagion, toxins, environmental hazards, aging); Disability and impairment (sensory loss, mobility limitations, cognitive deficits)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human conceptualization of bodily structure, including the recognition, naming, and understanding of body parts, their spatial relationships, and their functional organization. It captures both external anatomy visible to everyday observation and internal structures understood through embodied experience.
Exemplary Concepts: External body parts (head, limbs, torso, facial features, skin, hair); Internal organs (heart, lungs, brain, stomach, liver, kidneys); Body systems (skeletal, muscular, nervous, circulatory, digestive); Sensory organs (eyes, ears, nose, mouth, skin receptors); Body cavities and spaces (chest, abdomen, joints, body openings); Body proportions (symmetry, size relationships, anatomical landmarks); Gender-specific anatomy (reproductive organs, secondary sex characteristics); Developmental anatomy (infant, child, adult, elderly body configurations)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of the ongoing biological processes that maintain life and enable bodily functions. It encompasses the recognition and conceptualization of automatic and voluntary bodily processes, from basic vital functions to complex coordinated activities.
Exemplary Concepts: Vital functions (heartbeat, breathing, circulation, digestion, excretion); Sensory processes (seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, touching); Motor functions (walking, grasping, balance, coordination, reflexes); Regulatory processes (temperature control, sleep-wake cycles, hunger-satiety); Cognitive functions (attention, memory, perception, consciousness); Reproductive functions (sexual arousal, menstruation, lactation, fertility); Immune responses (inflammation, wound healing, disease resistance); Developmental processes (growth spurts, puberty, aging changes)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of the interconnected relationships between living organisms and their environments, including concepts of habitats, ecosystems, and environmental interdependence. It captures how humans perceive and categorize the complex web of relationships that sustain life on multiple scales.
Exemplary Concepts: Habitat types (forests, grasslands, wetlands, deserts, marine environments); Ecological relationships (food chains, predator-prey, pollination, decomposition); Environmental cycles (seasonal changes, water cycle, nutrient cycling); Biodiversity patterns (species richness, endemic species, invasive species); Resource flows (energy transfer, material cycling, migration patterns); Environmental pressures (climate, competition, limited resources, natural disasters); Human-environment interaction (agriculture, conservation, pollution, habitat modification); Ecosystem services (pollination, water purification, climate regulation, soil formation)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of the various positions, functions, and identities that individuals occupy within social structures. It captures how humans conceptualize social personas, status positions, and the behavioral expectations associated with different social categories and relationships.
Exemplary Concepts: Family roles (parent, child, sibling, grandparent, spouse, in-law); Professional roles (teacher, doctor, manager, employee, entrepreneur, artist); Community roles (leader, elder, newcomer, neighbor, volunteer, citizen); Age-based identities (infant, child, teenager, adult, senior); Gender identities (masculine, feminine, non-binary expressions and roles); Social status markers (wealthy, poor, educated, prestigious, marginalized); Cultural identities (ethnic, religious, regional, national affiliations); Achieved vs. ascribed roles (earned positions vs. inherited status)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of formal and informal social structures that organize collective activities, establish rules, and coordinate group behavior. It encompasses how humans conceptualize, participate in, and navigate various organizational forms that structure social life.
Exemplary Concepts: Educational institutions (schools, universities, libraries, training centers); Religious organizations (churches, temples, monasteries, religious orders); Government bodies (legislatures, courts, bureaucracies, military, police); Economic institutions (markets, banks, corporations, trade unions, cooperatives); Healthcare systems (hospitals, clinics, insurance, medical associations); Cultural institutions (museums, theaters, media organizations, festivals); Social service organizations (charities, NGOs, community centers, support groups); Informal institutions (clubs, societies, networks, traditions, customs)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of exchange, trade, and economic relationships that involve the production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services. It captures how humans conceptualize value, transaction, and the various forms of economic interaction in their social environment.
Exemplary Concepts: Exchange mechanisms (buying, selling, trading, bartering, gifting, sharing); Financial concepts (money, credit, debt, investment, savings, insurance); Market activities (shopping, negotiating, advertising, competing, pricing); Work and labor (employment, wages, contracts, business ownership, entrepreneurship); Economic relationships (customer-vendor, employer-employee, landlord-tenant); Value concepts (cost, profit, loss, worth, affordability, luxury vs. necessity); Economic institutions (banking, taxation, property rights, market regulation); Resource management (budgeting, planning, allocation, conservation, waste)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of systems of authority, rule-making, and social control that regulate collective behavior. It encompasses how humans conceptualize legitimate authority, legal obligations, and the various mechanisms societies use to establish and enforce social order.
Exemplary Concepts: Authority structures (leadership, hierarchy, sovereignty, jurisdiction, mandate); Legal concepts (laws, rights, obligations, contracts, property, citizenship); Justice systems (courts, trials, judges, juries, punishment, rehabilitation); Enforcement mechanisms (police, military, penalties, sanctions, compliance); Political processes (voting, representation, legislation, policy-making, advocacy); Regulatory frameworks (permits, licenses, standards, inspections, violations); Dispute resolution (mediation, arbitration, litigation, negotiation, appeals); Constitutional concepts (fundamental rights, separation of powers, checks and balances)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses the dynamic processes through which humans connect, communicate, and form relationships with one another. It captures the various forms of social engagement, from brief encounters to deep personal bonds, and the behavioral patterns that characterize human social connection.
Exemplary Concepts: Relationship types (friendship, romance, marriage, kinship, mentorship, rivalry); Social behaviors (greeting, conversation, cooperation, competition, helping, sharing); Communication patterns (talking, listening, arguing, persuading, comforting, joking); Emotional connections (love, trust, loyalty, jealousy, attachment, intimacy); Social networks (family circles, friend groups, professional networks, communities); Relationship dynamics (bonding, conflict, reconciliation, separation, reunion); Social rituals (introductions, celebrations, ceremonies, farewells, traditions); Group membership (inclusion, exclusion, belonging, acceptance, rejection)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of the spectrum from collaborative to competitive social dynamics, including the recognition, management, and resolution of social tensions. It encompasses both constructive cooperation and destructive conflict as fundamental patterns of human social interaction.
Exemplary Concepts: Cooperative behaviors (teamwork, collaboration, mutual aid, alliance-building, sharing resources); Competitive dynamics (rivalry, competition, contest, struggle for resources or status); Conflict types (arguments, disputes, feuds, wars, legal battles, ideological conflicts); Resolution mechanisms (negotiation, compromise, mediation, peace-making, reconciliation); Alliance patterns (partnership, coalition, treaty, mutual defense, joint ventures); Aggressive behaviors (fighting, bullying, intimidation, retaliation, revenge); Peaceful coexistence (tolerance, diplomacy, neutrality, peaceful settlement); Power struggles (dominance, submission, resistance, rebellion, revolution)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of how groups form, function, and influence individual behavior. It captures the emergent properties of collective action, group psychology, and the various ways that being part of a group changes individual cognition and behavior.
Exemplary Concepts: Group formation (joining, initiation, membership criteria, group identity, solidarity); Collective action (protests, movements, mob behavior, crowd dynamics, mass events); Group decision-making (consensus, voting, deliberation, leadership, influence); Social pressure (conformity, peer pressure, social norms, groupthink, dissent); Group roles (leaders, followers, facilitators, disruptors, outsiders, scapegoats); Collective identity (team spirit, group loyalty, in-group/out-group distinctions); Mass phenomena (trends, fashions, panics, rumors, collective memory); Organizational behavior (workplace culture, team dynamics, institutional culture)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of fundamental perspectives on reality, existence, and meaning that shape how individuals and groups interpret their world. It captures the deep cognitive frameworks, belief systems, and philosophical orientations that guide human understanding of life's fundamental questions and provide interpretive lenses for experience.
Exemplary Concepts: Religious beliefs (monotheism, polytheism, animism, atheism, agnosticism, spirituality); Cosmological views (creation stories, afterlife concepts, divine intervention, fate vs. free will); Philosophical orientations (materialism, idealism, existentialism, determinism, humanism); Explanatory frameworks (scientific rationalism, mysticism, traditionalism, supernaturalism); Value systems (individualism vs. collectivism, progress vs. tradition, sacred vs. secular); Meaning-making concepts (purpose of life, suffering, justice, truth, beauty, goodness); Epistemological beliefs (how knowledge is acquired, sources of authority, truth criteria); Metaphysical concepts (nature of reality, consciousness, time, causation, unity vs. diversity)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of ceremonial practices, customary behaviors, and repeated cultural activities that mark significant events, maintain social bonds, and transmit cultural knowledge. It encompasses both formal religious ceremonies and informal cultural practices that structure social life through symbolic action.
Exemplary Concepts: Life cycle rituals (birth ceremonies, coming of age, weddings, funerals, baptisms); Religious ceremonies (worship services, prayer rituals, pilgrimage, communion, meditation); Seasonal celebrations (harvest festivals, new year celebrations, solstice observances); Community traditions (parades, festivals, commemorations, reunions, local customs); Daily rituals (meal practices, greeting customs, bedtime routines, work traditions); Ceremonial objects (sacred items, regalia, ceremonial tools, symbolic artifacts); Rites of passage (initiation ceremonies, graduation, ordination, retirement celebrations); Commemorative practices (memorial services, anniversary observances, historical reenactments)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of creative expression, aesthetic appreciation, and the various forms of artistic production that communicate meaning, emotion, and cultural values through symbolic representation. It captures how humans conceptualize beauty, creativity, and the role of artistic expression in cultural life.
Exemplary Concepts: Visual arts (painting, sculpture, drawing, photography, crafts, decorative arts); Performing arts (music, dance, theater, storytelling, opera, circus); Literary arts (poetry, fiction, drama, oral literature, folklore, epic narratives); Media arts (film, television, digital art, multimedia installations, video games); Craft traditions (pottery, weaving, woodworking, metalworking, jewelry making); Aesthetic concepts (beauty, style, artistic movement, genre, technique, artistic vision); Creative processes (inspiration, composition, performance, interpretation, criticism); Cultural expression (folk art, popular culture, high art, avant-garde, traditional forms)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of the stories, histories, and collective memories that communities use to understand their past, define their identity, and guide their future. It encompasses how humans create, maintain, and transmit shared narratives that provide cultural continuity and meaning.
Exemplary Concepts: Historical narratives (origin stories, founding myths, historical events, legendary figures); Collective memory (shared experiences, cultural trauma, golden ages, formative events); Oral traditions (folktales, legends, proverbs, genealogies, traditional stories); Cultural heroes (mythical figures, historical leaders, cultural icons, role models); Cautionary tales (moral stories, warnings, examples of consequences, wisdom literature); Identity narratives (cultural uniqueness, shared struggles, community achievements); Explanatory stories (origin myths, creation accounts, aetiological tales); Commemorative practices (storytelling traditions, historical preservation, memory keeping)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of cultural inheritance, group membership, and the various markers that define cultural identity and belonging. It captures how humans conceptualize their connection to cultural traditions, ancestral communities, and the symbols that represent cultural distinctiveness.
Exemplary Concepts: Ethnic identity (ancestry, bloodlines, tribal affiliation, ethnic pride, cultural roots); Cultural symbols (flags, emblems, traditional clothing, cultural icons, sacred objects); Language heritage (native tongues, dialects, linguistic identity, mother language); Cultural practices (traditional foods, customs, celebrations, inherited knowledge); Ancestral connections (family lineage, genealogy, ancestral homeland, inherited status); Cultural preservation (maintaining traditions, cultural revival, heritage protection); Identity markers (names, cultural dress, religious affiliation, regional identity); Cultural transmission (passing down traditions, teaching cultural knowledge, intergenerational learning)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of the processes through which sensory information is received, interpreted, and organized into meaningful experience. It captures how humans conceptualize the interface between their consciousness and the external world through the various sensory modalities and perceptual processes.
Exemplary Concepts: Sensory modalities (vision, hearing, touch, taste, smell, proprioception, balance); Perceptual qualities (brightness, color, loudness, pitch, texture, temperature, flavor); Spatial perception (depth, distance, size, shape, orientation, movement detection); Temporal perception (duration, rhythm, sequence, simultaneity, change detection); Perceptual organization (figure-ground, pattern recognition, gestalt principles, grouping); Sensory thresholds (detection limits, discrimination abilities, sensitivity variations); Perceptual illusions (optical illusions, auditory illusions, misperceptions, hallucinations); Cross-modal perception (synesthesia, sensory substitution, multisensory integration)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of feeling states, emotional responses, and the complex affective experiences that color human consciousness. It encompasses how humans recognize, categorize, and experience the full spectrum of emotions and moods that influence cognition, behavior, and social interaction.
Exemplary Concepts: Basic emotions (joy, sadness, anger, fear, disgust, surprise, contempt, love); Complex emotions (guilt, shame, pride, envy, gratitude, nostalgia, awe, empathy); Mood states (depression, elation, irritability, contentment, anxiety, excitement); Emotional intensity (mild feelings, intense passion, overwhelming emotion, emotional numbness); Affective qualities (pleasant vs. unpleasant, arousing vs. calming, approach vs. avoidance); Emotional regulation (control, expression, suppression, amplification, coping strategies); Social emotions (embarrassment, jealousy, compassion, moral outrage, social anxiety); Emotional development (emotional maturity, emotional intelligence, feeling recognition)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of the internal forces that energize, direct, and sustain behavior toward goals. It captures how humans conceptualize the various biological and psychological needs, drives, and motivational states that influence action, decision-making, and goal pursuit.
Exemplary Concepts: Biological needs (hunger, thirst, sleep, sexual desire, pain avoidance, homeostatic drives); Psychological needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness, security, achievement, recognition); Social motivations (belonging, status, approval, intimacy, power, influence); Growth motivations (self-actualization, learning, creativity, meaning-seeking, spiritual fulfillment); Motivational states (desire, craving, ambition, passion, determination, apathy); Goal orientations (approach vs. avoidance, intrinsic vs. extrinsic, short-term vs. long-term); Drive regulation (impulse control, delayed gratification, habit formation, addiction); Motivational conflicts (competing desires, approach-avoidance conflicts, value conflicts)

Subdomain: Subjective Experience of the Body and Environment
Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of the felt, phenomenological aspects of embodied existence and environmental experience. It encompasses how humans conceptualize their immediate, first-person experience of being a conscious body situated in a physical and social environment.
Exemplary Concepts: Bodily sensations (comfort, discomfort, tension, relaxation, energy, fatigue, vitality); Interoceptive awareness (heartbeat, breathing, hunger, thirst, internal bodily signals); Spatial embodiment (body schema, personal space, embodied presence, spatial orientation); Environmental presence (sense of place, atmosphere, environmental mood, spatial qualities); Phenomenological states (flow states, altered consciousness, peak experiences, mindfulness); Somatic responses (gut feelings, embodied intuition, physical reactions to emotion); Temporal embodiment (sense of duration, temporal flow, present moment awareness); Relational embodiment (interpersonal presence, social attunement, embodied empathy)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of consciousness, selective attention, and the various states and processes through which humans become aware of and focus on particular aspects of their internal or external environment. It captures how humans conceptualize the dynamic, selective nature of conscious awareness and attentional control.
Exemplary Concepts: Attentional focus (concentration, selective attention, divided attention, sustained attention); Awareness states (consciousness, mindfulness, alertness, vigilance, absent-mindedness); Attentional control (focusing, shifting attention, filtering distractions, mental effort); Levels of awareness (conscious, preconscious, unconscious, subliminal, peripheral awareness); Attentional scope (narrow focus, broad awareness, tunnel vision, global attention); Meta-awareness (awareness of awareness, attention to attention, metacognitive monitoring); Altered states (meditation, flow states, daydreaming, hypnosis, absorption); Attentional disorders (distractibility, hypervigilance, attention deficits, obsessive focus)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of how information is acquired, stored, retrieved, and modified over time. It encompasses the various forms of memory and learning processes through which humans accumulate knowledge, develop skills, and benefit from experience to guide future behavior and understanding.
Exemplary Concepts: Memory types (episodic, semantic, procedural, working memory, long-term memory, short-term memory); Learning processes (acquisition, practice, rehearsal, consolidation, skill development); Memory phenomena (forgetting, remembering, recognition, recall, false memories, flashbulb memories); Learning strategies (repetition, association, elaboration, organization, retrieval practice); Memory cues (reminders, associations, context, priming, retrieval cues); Knowledge organization (schemas, categories, mental models, conceptual frameworks); Expertise development (novice to expert progression, automaticity, domain knowledge); Memory failures (amnesia, confusion, tip-of-tongue, interference, decay)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of logical thinking, analytical processes, and the systematic approaches used to work through complex problems and arrive at conclusions. It captures how humans conceptualize rational thought, inference-making, and the various strategies for overcoming cognitive challenges.
Exemplary Concepts: Logical reasoning (deduction, induction, abduction, syllogistic reasoning, logical validity); Problem-solving strategies (trial and error, means-ends analysis, working backward, analogical reasoning); Analytical thinking (breaking down problems, identifying patterns, systematic analysis); Creative problem-solving (brainstorming, lateral thinking, insight, innovative solutions); Reasoning errors (logical fallacies, cognitive biases, faulty assumptions, circular reasoning); Evidence evaluation (weighing evidence, assessing credibility, identifying relevant information); Abstract thinking (conceptual reasoning, hypothetical thinking, counterfactual reasoning); Mathematical reasoning (quantitative thinking, mathematical proof, statistical reasoning, numerical relationships)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of choice processes, future-oriented thinking, and the various mechanisms through which humans select among alternatives and organize actions toward future goals. It encompasses both immediate decision-making and long-term strategic planning.
Exemplary Concepts: Choice processes (option generation, alternative evaluation, preference formation, commitment); Planning activities (goal setting, strategy formation, resource allocation, timeline development); Decision factors (costs and benefits, risks and rewards, uncertainty, value tradeoffs); Temporal considerations (short-term vs. long-term thinking, delayed gratification, future discounting); Strategic thinking (anticipating consequences, considering multiple scenarios, contingency planning); Decision aids (decision trees, pros and cons lists, advice seeking, deliberation strategies); Planning tools (schedules, priorities, milestones, resource management, coordination); Choice difficulties (decision paralysis, analysis paralysis, regret, satisficing vs. optimizing)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of the information, beliefs, and assumptions that constitute their knowledge base and worldview. It captures how humans conceptualize what they know, how they know it, and the various forms of knowledge and belief that guide their understanding and behavior.
Exemplary Concepts: Knowledge types (factual knowledge, procedural knowledge, experiential knowledge, tacit knowledge); Belief systems (core beliefs, peripheral beliefs, religious beliefs, political beliefs, scientific beliefs); Epistemic states (certainty, doubt, confidence, skepticism, conviction, open-mindedness); Knowledge sources (experience, education, authority, intuition, tradition, evidence); Assumptions (background assumptions, default beliefs, implicit knowledge, taken-for-granted truths); Belief revision (learning, updating beliefs, changing minds, paradigm shifts, cognitive dissonance); Knowledge organization (mental models, frameworks, theories, conceptual structures); Epistemic virtues (intellectual humility, curiosity, critical thinking, open inquiry)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of purposive behavior, goal formation, and the various processes through which humans translate desires and intentions into organized action. It encompasses how humans conceptualize agency, willpower, and the relationship between mental intentions and behavioral execution.
Exemplary Concepts: Goal formation (desire, intention, commitment, goal setting, aspiration, ambition); Action planning (implementation intentions, action sequences, means-ends chains, strategy selection); Volitional control (willpower, self-control, effort, persistence, resistance to temptation); Goal management (goal pursuit, goal switching, goal abandonment, prioritization); Intentional states (trying, striving, attempting, endeavoring, working toward); Action monitoring (progress tracking, performance evaluation, course correction, adaptation); Goal conflicts (competing goals, approach-avoidance conflicts, value conflicts, resource allocation); Agency experiences (sense of control, self-efficacy, personal causation, empowerment)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of assessment processes, evaluative thinking, and the various ways humans form judgments about quality, value, truth, and significance. It captures how humans conceptualize evaluation, criticism, and the processes through which they assess and compare different options, ideas, or situations.
Exemplary Concepts: Quality assessments (good vs. bad, excellent vs. poor, superior vs. inferior, adequate vs. inadequate); Value judgments (important vs. trivial, worthwhile vs. worthless, significant vs. insignificant); Truth evaluations (true vs. false, accurate vs. inaccurate, valid vs. invalid, credible vs. dubious); Aesthetic judgments (beautiful vs. ugly, elegant vs. crude, artistic vs. artless, tasteful vs. tasteless); Comparative assessments (better vs. worse, more vs. less, superior vs. inferior, preferable vs. undesirable); Appropriateness judgments (suitable vs. unsuitable, proper vs. improper, fitting vs. unfitting); Evaluation criteria (standards, benchmarks, expectations, requirements, ideals); Judgment processes (critical thinking, assessment strategies, evaluation frameworks, bias recognition)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of symbolic systems, communicative processes, and the various means through which humans create, transmit, and interpret meaningful information. It captures how humans conceptualize language, signs, symbols, and the full spectrum of communicative acts that enable shared understanding and cultural transmission.
Exemplary Concepts: Language systems (spoken language, written language, sign language, grammar, vocabulary, syntax); Symbolic communication (gestures, facial expressions, body language, pictograms, icons, symbols); Communicative acts (speaking, listening, reading, writing, signing, storytelling, explaining); Meaning-making (interpretation, understanding, translation, disambiguation, context); Communication channels (verbal, nonverbal, visual, auditory, tactile, digital); Discourse types (conversation, narrative, argument, description, instruction, poetry); Pragmatic dimensions (tone, intent, context, audience, appropriateness, effectiveness); Communication problems (misunderstanding, ambiguity, language barriers, communication breakdown)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of how information is organized, categorized, and structured to enable efficient storage, retrieval, and manipulation. It encompasses the various ways humans conceptualize the arrangement and relationship of information elements, from simple lists to complex hierarchical and network structures.
Exemplary Concepts: Organizational structures (hierarchies, networks, matrices, trees, graphs, taxonomies); Categorization systems (classification, typologies, schemas, frameworks, inventories); Information relationships (associations, dependencies, causation, similarity, sequence); Data formats (lists, tables, databases, documents, files, records, entries); Structural elements (headers, sections, chapters, indices, cross-references, links); Organization principles (alphabetical, chronological, topical, hierarchical, geographical); Information architecture (navigation, search, filtering, sorting, grouping, tagging); Knowledge organization (ontologies, concept maps, mental models, semantic networks)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of technological systems for creating, transmitting, and manipulating information and representations. It captures how humans conceptualize digital technologies, electronic media, and the various technological tools that extend human representational and communicative capabilities.
Exemplary Concepts: Digital technologies (computers, smartphones, internet, software, applications, platforms); Media formats (text, audio, video, images, multimedia, interactive media, virtual reality); Communication technologies (email, messaging, social media, video conferencing, forums); Information processing (data entry, editing, searching, filtering, manipulation, analysis); Digital interfaces (screens, keyboards, touchscreens, voice commands, gesture controls); Network systems (internet, wireless, cloud computing, distributed systems, connectivity); Digital content (websites, digital documents, databases, digital libraries, online resources); Technology interaction (user experience, digital literacy, human-computer interaction, automation)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of systematic, rule-based representational systems that use abstract symbols and logical relationships to model reality or manipulate information. It encompasses mathematical systems, logical frameworks, and other formal representational tools that enable precise, systematic thinking and analysis.
Exemplary Concepts: Mathematical systems (arithmetic, algebra, geometry, calculus, statistics, mathematical logic); Logical systems (propositional logic, predicate logic, modal logic, reasoning systems); Formal notations (mathematical notation, logical symbols, scientific formulas, programming languages); Abstract models (mathematical models, theoretical frameworks, formal theories, axiomatic systems); Systematic procedures (algorithms, proofs, calculations, derivations, systematic methods); Rule-based systems (formal rules, syntactic systems, transformation rules, inference rules); Precision tools (measurement systems, quantification, exactness, formal definitions); Abstract relationships (functions, mappings, equivalences, transformations, isomorphisms)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of how knowledge, concepts, and meanings are encoded, stored, and expressed in various representational formats. It captures how humans conceptualize the relationship between mental content and its external representation, including the various ways knowledge can be captured, organized, and communicated.
Exemplary Concepts: Representational formats (concepts, definitions, descriptions, explanations, models, theories); Knowledge structures (schemas, frameworks, ontologies, concept maps, knowledge graphs); Semantic relationships (is-a relationships, part-whole, cause-effect, similarity, opposition); Conceptual organization (categories, hierarchies, networks, associations, semantic fields); Knowledge encoding (documentation, notation, representation languages, knowledge bases); Meaning representation (semantic content, propositional knowledge, conceptual knowledge); Knowledge artifacts (encyclopedias, textbooks, manuals, reference works, knowledge systems); Representational adequacy (completeness, accuracy, clarity, usability, expressiveness)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of the arrangement, organization, and geometric relationships of objects and spaces. It captures how humans conceptualize spatial structures, layouts, and the various ways that entities can be positioned, oriented, and arranged in relation to one another within spatial frameworks.
Exemplary Concepts: Geometric relationships (parallel, perpendicular, intersecting, adjacent, overlapping, concentric); Spatial arrangements (patterns, layouts, configurations, formations, alignments, distributions); Dimensional concepts (one-dimensional, two-dimensional, three-dimensional, height, width, depth); Architectural spaces (rooms, buildings, courtyards, corridors, levels, compartments); Landscape features (terrain, topography, elevation, slopes, valleys, ridges, boundaries); Positional relationships (inside-outside, above-below, front-back, left-right, center-periphery); Spatial scales (microscopic, human-scale, architectural, geographical, cosmic); Organizational principles (symmetry, balance, proportion, hierarchy, zoning, clustering)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of how time is organized, structured, and experienced, including the various ways humans conceptualize temporal relationships, sequences, rhythms, and the organization of events within temporal frameworks. It encompasses both subjective time experience and objective temporal organization.
Exemplary Concepts: Temporal units (moments, seconds, minutes, hours, days, weeks, months, years, decades); Temporal cycles (daily rhythms, weekly patterns, seasonal cycles, annual cycles, life cycles); Temporal organization (schedules, calendars, timetables, agendas, chronologies, timelines); Temporal relationships (before, after, during, simultaneous, sequential, overlapping); Temporal patterns (rhythms, periodicities, frequencies, intervals, repetitions, variations); Time periods (past, present, future, eras, epochs, ages, periods, phases); Temporal boundaries (beginnings, endings, transitions, deadlines, milestones, turning points); Subjective time (duration experience, time perception, temporal flow, temporal perspective)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of movement through space and time, including the paths, patterns, and dynamics of motion as experienced and conceptualized by humans. It captures how humans perceive, predict, and understand the temporal-spatial aspects of movement and the trajectories that moving entities follow.
Exemplary Concepts: Movement patterns (straight-line motion, curved paths, circular motion, oscillation, spiral motion); Trajectory characteristics (direction, speed, acceleration, velocity, momentum, inertia); Movement types (translation, rotation, vibration, undulation, bouncing, flowing); Path properties (smooth, jagged, predictable, random, efficient, indirect, optimal); Directional concepts (toward, away from, north, south, up, down, forward, backward); Dynamic relationships (following, leading, converging, diverging, intercepting, avoiding); Spatial-temporal integration (distance over time, position changes, route planning, navigation); Movement prediction (trajectory extrapolation, collision prediction, arrival time estimation)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of transformation, development, and evolution as processes that unfold through time. It encompasses how humans conceptualize various forms of change, from gradual development to sudden transformation, and the temporal patterns that characterize different types of change processes.
Exemplary Concepts: Change patterns (gradual change, sudden change, cyclical change, exponential change, linear change); Developmental processes (growth, maturation, aging, evolution, progress, regression); Transformation types (improvement, deterioration, adaptation, metamorphosis, revolution, reform); Temporal dynamics (acceleration, deceleration, persistence, fluctuation, stability, volatility); Change trajectories (upward trends, downward trends, plateaus, cycles, oscillations); Causal sequences (cause and effect over time, chain reactions, cascading effects, feedback loops); Life processes (birth, development, reproduction, death, regeneration, renewal); Historical patterns (trends, eras, epochs, transitions, turning points, continuities)

Subdomain: Conventions and Ethical Norms and Principles
Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of the rules, standards, and principles that guide moral behavior and define what is considered right, wrong, good, or bad within communities and cultures. It captures how humans conceptualize ethical frameworks, moral codes, and the normative systems that prescribe appropriate conduct and character.
Exemplary Concepts: Ethical principles (honesty, fairness, compassion, integrity, respect, responsibility, loyalty); Moral rules (prohibitions against harm, requirements of care, duties of reciprocity); Virtue concepts (courage, temperance, wisdom, justice, kindness, humility, patience); Vice concepts (greed, cruelty, dishonesty, cowardice, pride, selfishness, injustice); Normative frameworks (deontological duties, consequentialist calculations, virtue ethics); Social conventions (politeness, etiquette, propriety, decorum, appropriate behavior); Professional ethics (medical ethics, business ethics, research ethics, legal ethics); Universal vs. relative (cross-cultural principles vs. culturally specific norms)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of the processes through which moral assessments are made, including the evaluation of actions, intentions, character, and outcomes according to moral criteria. It encompasses how humans conceptualize moral reasoning, ethical decision-making, and the formation of moral judgments about rightness and wrongness.
Exemplary Concepts: Moral evaluations (right vs. wrong, good vs. evil, virtuous vs. vicious, praiseworthy vs. blameworthy); Judgment processes (moral reasoning, ethical deliberation, conscience, moral intuition); Moral considerations (intentions, consequences, character, context, circumstances); Ethical dilemmas (competing values, tragic choices, moral conflicts, ethical trade-offs); Moral responsibility (accountability, culpability, praise, blame, moral agency); Judgment criteria (harm, fairness, rights, dignity, consequences, duties, virtues); Moral complexity (moral ambiguity, ethical uncertainty, competing claims, contextual factors); Moral development (moral maturity, ethical growth, moral learning, conscience formation)

Definition: This subdomain encompasses human understanding of entitlements, obligations, and fair treatment within social relationships and institutional arrangements. It captures how humans conceptualize what individuals owe to each other, what they are entitled to receive, and what constitutes fair distribution of benefits, burdens, and treatment.
Exemplary Concepts: Individual rights (human rights, civil rights, privacy, autonomy, freedom, dignity); Social duties (obligations to others, civic duties, role responsibilities, care obligations); Distributive justice (fair allocation, equality, equity, merit-based distribution, need-based distribution); Procedural justice (fair processes, due process, equal treatment, impartial procedures); Reciprocity concepts (mutual obligation, give and take, balance, exchange, reciprocal duties); Entitlement concepts (deserving, earning, merit, need-based claims, basic entitlements); Justice institutions (legal systems, courts, arbitration, conflict resolution, enforcement); Social contracts (mutual agreements, collective obligations, social cooperation, shared responsibility)

Definition: This subdomain captures human understanding of the emotional dimensions of moral experience, including the feelings that accompany moral judgments, moral violations, and moral achievements. It encompasses how humans conceptualize guilt, shame, pride, indignation, empathy, and other emotions that are specifically connected to moral situations and moral agency.
Exemplary Concepts: Self-directed moral emotions (guilt, shame, pride, regret, moral satisfaction, self-respect); Other-directed moral emotions (anger, indignation, contempt, admiration, gratitude, forgiveness); Empathetic responses (compassion, sympathy, concern, caring, emotional resonance); Moral motivation (moral outrage, righteous anger, moral passion, ethical commitment); Conscience experiences (moral qualms, ethical unease, moral clarity, inner voice); Moral repair (remorse, repentance, apology, forgiveness, reconciliation, redemption); Moral inspiration (moral elevation, ethical admiration, moral beauty, noble emotions); Moral distress (moral injury, ethical stress, moral conflict, value conflicts)
